---
title: "Twitch"
output: pdf_document
date: "2025-09-25"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyverse)
library(forcats)
library(ggcorrplot)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## Dataset
```{r}
twitch.streamers <- read.csv("./twitch-streamers.csv")
head(twitch.streamers)
summary(twitch.streamers)
```

## A Preliminary Exploration of the Twitch Streamer Dataset

```{r}
top.twitch.streamers <- 
  twitch.streamers[,c("NAME", "TOTAL_FOLLOWERS")] %>% 
  arrange(desc(TOTAL_FOLLOWERS))
head(top.twitch.streamers, 100)


  
  
  
streamers.language <- twitch.streamers %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = fct_infreq(LANGUAGE))) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust=1))+
  labs(title ="Top 1000 Twitch Streamers per Language")+
  xlab("Language")+
  ylab("Count") +
  geom_bar(fill = "red")
streamers.language

```

Suppose we want to build a model which could predict the number of viewers a 
streamer would get on average. First, some of our observations shows 0 average 
viewers per stream. According to the author of this dataset, this is due to 
missing or inacessible data, not that the true number of viewers is actually zero.
To make the model more accurate, we should remove these data points from out
data frame.
```{r}
twitch.streamers.clean <- twitch.streamers %>% 
  filter(AVG_VIEWERS_PER_STREAM != 0)

head(twitch.streamers.clean,50)
```

A good place to start would be to analyze the relationship between the number of
Twitch followers and average viewers per stream.  Below is a scatterplot detailing
this realtionship.  We can see that there is a positive relationship between these
two factors.

```{r}

followers.v.meanviewers <- twitch.streamers.clean %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = TOTAL_FOLLOWERS, y = AVG_VIEWERS_PER_STREAM)) +
  theme_bw() +
  #theme(plot.margin = margin(0.5,0.75,0.5,0.5, "cm")) + 
  ggtitle("Number of Followers vs Average views per Stream") +
  scale_y_continuous(trans="log10", labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_x_continuous(trans = "log10", labels = scales::comma) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)
  
followers.v.meanviewers
```
If we do a simple regression analysis, we can see that we result in a p-value of 
nearly 0, meaning we can reject the nul: the number of followers has a statistically
significant effect on the average views per stream. For every 100 followers, we can
predict 1 extra average viewer per stream.
```{r}
mean.viewers <- lm(AVG_VIEWERS_PER_STREAM ~ TOTAL_FOLLOWERS, data = twitch.streamers.clean)
summary(mean.viewers)

```
Suppose we want to make a multilinear model, however. We can start by making a
model using more of our continous factors: Total Views, Total time streamed,
per week, and average stream duration.  First, we want to confirm the multicollinearity
between these factors and if these factors share a linear relationship with 
average viewers per stream.  We can see that the average stream duration 
and active days per week does not seem to have a linear relationship, so we can 
drop these from the model.  Total time streamed also seems to have a tenous
relationship with average viewers.  This was a bit of a surprise to me because my
assumptions going into this analysis was that people who have been streaming for
a long time would attract more viewers on average, but this seems not to be the case.
Each of our correlations are less than 0.8, so we 
need not remove any variables through multicolinearity alone. However, a correlation
coefficient of 0.65 is still quite high, so we should keep this in mind.
Considering these graphs, we should add only total views to our model.

```{r}
TotalViews.v.meanviewers <- twitch.streamers.clean %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = TOTAL_VIEWS, y = AVG_VIEWERS_PER_STREAM)) +
  theme_bw() +
  #theme(plot.margin = margin(0.5,0.75,0.5,0.5, "cm")) + 
  ggtitle("Total Views vs Average views per Stream") +
  scale_y_continuous(trans="log10", labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_x_continuous(trans = "log10", labels = scales::comma) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)

StreamDuration.v.meanviewers <- twitch.streamers.clean %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = AVERAGE_STREAM_DURATION, y = AVG_VIEWERS_PER_STREAM)) +
  theme_bw() +
  #theme(plot.margin = margin(0.5,0.75,0.5,0.5, "cm")) + 
  ggtitle("Average stream duration vs Average views per Stream") +
  #scale_y_continuous(trans="log10", labels = scales::comma) +
  #scale_x_continuous(trans = "log10", labels = scales::comma) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)


ActiveDays.v.meanviewers <- twitch.streamers.clean %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = ACTIVE_DAYS_PER_WEEK, y = AVG_VIEWERS_PER_STREAM)) +
  theme_bw() +
  #theme(plot.margin = margin(0.5,0.75,0.5,0.5, "cm")) + 
  ggtitle("Active Days Per Week vs Average views per Stream") +
  #scale_y_continuous(trans="log10", labels = scales::comma) +
  #scale_x_continuous(trans = "log10", labels = scales::comma) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)

TimeStreamed.v.meanviewers <- twitch.streamers.clean %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = TOTAL_TIME_STREAMED, y = AVG_VIEWERS_PER_STREAM)) +
  theme_bw() +
  #theme(plot.margin = margin(0.5,0.75,0.5,0.5, "cm")) + 
  ggtitle("Total Time Streamed vs Average views per Stream") +
  scale_y_continuous(trans="log10", labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_x_continuous(trans = "log10", labels = scales::comma) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)


TotalViews.v.meanviewers
StreamDuration.v.meanviewers
ActiveDays.v.meanviewers
TimeStreamed.v.meanviewers


twitch.streamers.reduced <- twitch.streamers.clean %>%  subset(select = c(
  TOTAL_FOLLOWERS, ACTIVE_DAYS_PER_WEEK, TOTAL_VIEWS,TOTAL_TIME_STREAMED,AVERAGE_STREAM_DURATION))

twitch.corr <- cor(twitch.streamers.reduced)
ggcorrplot(twitch.corr, hc.order = TRUE, type = "lower", lab = TRUE) 


```

Adding these two variables to the model, we can see that the p-value for the
number of followers has increased significantly. This is likely due to the
high correlation between total views and total followers.  Both of our p-values are
still below our significance level of 0.05 however. Using ANOVA to compare our reduced
and full model, we result in a p-value of nearly 0, indicating we can reject the null:
Our full model is a better fit than our reduced one.
```{r}
mean.viewers.full <- lm(AVG_VIEWERS_PER_STREAM ~ TOTAL_FOLLOWERS + TOTAL_VIEWS, data = twitch.streamers.clean)
mean.viewers.full2 <- lm(AVG_VIEWERS_PER_STREAM ~ TOTAL_VIEWS + TOTAL_TIME_STREAMED, 
                        data = twitch.streamers.clean)


summary(mean.viewers.full)


anova(mean.viewers, mean.viewers.full)
```
We should also check if our residuals are normally distributed. In both of our 
models, we can see that our qqplots have promising results. Most of our residual
values fall in a straight line, indicating our residuals are normally distributed.

```{r}
qqnorm(mean.viewers$residuals)
qqline(mean.viewers$residuals)
qqnorm(mean.viewers.full$residuals)
qqline(mean.viewers.full$residuals)
```





## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:
